headline: BaseballPro77 Shatters the Record Books in Week 4, While a Deadlock at the Bottom Leaves Two Teams Searching for Answers
subheadline: Mike Trout's historic demolition powered a league-record 19-homer week, the Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs made the most impactful waiver moves of the season, and the Canberra Wombats and Riverside Roughnecks couldn't separate themselves from each other — or the middle of the standings.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

### Canberra Wombats vs. Riverside Roughnecks — 4-4 Tie

There are matchups that end in a draw and feel like a relief. This was not one of them. The **Canberra Wombats** and **Riverside Roughnecks** split all nine categories with neither team able to manufacture a decisive edge in any single area, producing the most frustrating possible outcome for two rosters still searching for an identity in the early going. The **Roughnecks** posted a superior .368 OBP and a strong NSVH contribution of 8, while the **Wombats** countered with a modest home run advantage and enough pitching to keep things level. A tie, in this context, satisfies no one.

The stakes for both teams are real. Neither club can afford to surrender ground in what is shaping up as a genuine bottom-half battle for playoff positioning. The **Roughnecks** boast legitimate pieces in Langeliers, Kurtz, and Misiorowski, but inconsistency across the lineup has prevented them from converting talent into wins. The **Wombats**, meanwhile, continue to be undermined by Gunnar Henderson's .189 OBP — a staggering underperformance from a player who carries significant roster investment — and a roster ceiling that remains in question.

A point split does nothing to resolve the questions surrounding either club. Both teams needed this one.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

There are statistical performances in fantasy baseball that demand acknowledgment the moment they appear on the ledger. What **BaseballPro77** accomplished in Week 4 falls into that category unambiguously.

The team posted 19 home runs and 39 RBI in a single week, breaking the single-week league records for both categories in the process. To put the power output in context: 19 home runs in seven days is a figure that would lead most real Major League Baseball teams on a weekly basis. The engine behind it all was Mike Trout, who authored a line that defies easy description — six home runs, 11 RBI, and a .472 OBP across the week. That is not a fantasy baseball performance. That is a video game simulation set to the lowest difficulty. Trout didn't just contribute to this team's record-breaking week. He was the record.

The final score against the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** was 7-2, and even that margin understates the degree of domination. **BaseballPro77** posted a 2.33 ERA on the pitching side, which means this was not a team winning with one hand while the other fell apart — this was a complete performance. The **Reapers**, to their credit, were not wholly without fight. They posted nine NSVH and received a genuine bright spot from Ben Rice. But they also managed only 33 hits, a .257 OBP, and a 5.01 ERA against the hottest roster in the league. Some matchups are simply decided before the first pitch.

With both the single-week HR and RBI records now firmly in **BaseballPro77**'s possession, the conversation about the league's most dangerous team is no longer a debate. It is a conclusion.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### BaseballPro77 def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 7-2

**BaseballPro77** didn't just win this matchup — they announced themselves as the preeminent force in the league. Their 19 HR and 39 RBI were historic by any measure, and the 2.33 ERA and 71 strikeouts over 69.2 innings demonstrated that the pitching staff is fully capable of holding a lead. The **Reapers** were undermined by a .257 OBP — which places them among the league's most vulnerable offensive rosters — and a 5.01 ERA that offered no path to a pitching-category comeback. Ben Rice continues to provide genuine value, but a 33-hit, low-OBP week reflects structural problems that one bright spot cannot resolve. Going forward, **BaseballPro77** enters Week 5 as the clear favorite in any matchup, while the **Reapers** face an urgent roster evaluation question: is the talent currently on this roster capable of turning around the offensive production, or does significant waiver activity need to follow?

### General Hospital def. Benchwarmers, 8-2

This was the most lopsided result of the week, and it was earned. **General Hospital** logged a league-best 8 category wins on the strength of 77.1 innings pitched, 6 quality starts, a 2.68 ERA, and a 0.96 WHIP — the latter figure representing elite-level pitching efficiency. Will Warren and the broader rotation have made this team quietly terrifying, and their offensive numbers — 35 runs, 10 HR, 36 RBI — were more than sufficient support. The **Benchwarmers**, for their part, received an extraordinary individual effort from Michael King, whose 19 strikeouts and 1.59 ERA were among the best individual pitching lines of the week. The problem is categorical and persistent: four home runs, 45 strikeouts surrendered, and bottom-five marks across nearly every offensive category indicate a roster that requires substantial restructuring, not minor adjustments. **General Hospital** looks like a legitimate title contender. The **Benchwarmers** look like a team with one elite piece and a collection of roster spots that need to be addressed before the standings become irreversible.

### Athletic Park Dragons def. North Correa, 5-2

The **Athletic Park Dragons** built this victory on a combination of Aaron Judge's extraordinary power output — six home runs in a single week, which would headline any matchup — and a pitching staff that is quietly becoming one of the league's most well-rounded. Cristopher Sánchez and Bailey Ober both looked sharp, contributing to a 3.01 ERA and 70 strikeouts over 68.2 innings. **North Correa** was not without contributions of their own: Chris Sale delivered an elite individual performance with a 0.69 ERA and 16 strikeouts, and the combination of Ohtani and Hoerner gives this team a legitimate offensive floor. But a 5.56 ERA outside of Sale remains the central vulnerability, and a team that posts 36 runs and 8 stolen bases yet still loses 5-2 is being beaten by pitching — which is the hardest kind of loss to reverse quickly. The **Dragons** are a team trending upward in all phases. The **Correa** squad needs to find reliable arms to complement their ace.

### Abbotsford-Colby Twins def. Hiroshima Radiation, 6-4

This was a victory built on offensive explosiveness against a team whose identity is built around the opposite. The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** posted 14 HR, 37 runs, and 8 stolen bases — a multi-category offensive assault led by a José Ramírez performance that included a remarkable .500 OBP and four home runs. The **Hiroshima Radiation**, as has been the pattern, pitched brilliantly — a 2.81 ERA, 1.09 WHIP, and 5 quality starts from McLean and Eovaldi and a deeper rotation represent genuine strength — but their offense managed only 6 HR, 30 runs, and a .307 OBP. When your pitching wins the pitching categories and you still lose the matchup 6-4, the offensive deficiency is not a minor concern — it is the defining challenge of the season. For the **Twins**, the result was deserved, though as the Lucky/Unlucky section notes, their expected output suggests even this win may not fully reflect how well they are actually performing.

### Albuquerque Bombers def. Sugar Land Skeeters, 5-4

This was the tightest result among the week's decided matchups, and it was not without intrigue. The **Albuquerque Bombers** prevailed on the strength of a 40-run week, 10 HR, 7 stolen bases, and a pitching line that held together well enough — 59 strikeouts, a 4.03 ERA, and 6 NSVH — to edge out a narrow win across the category scorecard. The **Sugar Land Skeeters** were genuinely competitive: Fernando Tatis Jr., Ian Happ, and Austin Weathers continue to provide multi-category upside, and the team's 72 strikeouts over 61 innings indicates a rotation that misses bats. The problem this week was a 6.05 ERA and 1.44 WHIP, which surrendered the pitching categories and proved to be the difference. The **Skeeters** remain the most consistent top-tier performer over the full four-week arc, and this loss reads more as a scheduling unfortunate than a structural concern. The **Bombers**, meanwhile, show genuine signs of a team growing into its potential through both in-game performance and aggressive roster management.

### Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs def. Buttville Skidmarks, 6-3

The **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** continued their pattern of pitching dominance with a league-leading 8 quality starts and a 2.49 ERA, anchored by Paul Skenes' exceptional debut contribution of a 0.90 ERA and 11 strikeouts in his first week on the roster. The Wonderdogs' 0.98 WHIP also reflects a staff that is not merely preventing runs — it is controlling games from the first inning. The **Buttville Skidmarks** suffered from a now-familiar dysfunction: Bryan Reynolds produced an extraordinary .500 OBP that, once again, received essentially no structural support from the surrounding roster. A 4.87 ERA and 1.48 WHIP from the pitching staff meant the **Skidmarks** were always running uphill. Reynolds' individual production is legitimately elite. The team around him is not, and at some point the gap between one player's performance and the roster's collective output becomes an indictment of roster construction.

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Benchwarmers**

It bears acknowledging, even amid the **Benchwarmers**' 8-2 loss, that the fortune metrics tell an interesting story. Despite posting bottom-of-the-league marks in home runs (4), strikeouts allowed (45), and hits, the **Benchwarmers** generated a 1.4 actual win contribution against an expected 0.19 — the largest positive luck delta in the league this week. Their competitive positioning against **General Hospital** was made marginally more favorable by matchup circumstance than their raw numbers would suggest they deserved. The takeaway is not that the **Benchwarmers** are secretly a stronger team than the box score indicates — they are not — but rather that even in a week defined by poor offensive production, the schedule and category distribution broke in their favor more than probability would have predicted.

**Unlucky Team: Abbotsford-Colby Twins**

The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** won their matchup, which makes their appearance in this section somewhat counterintuitive. But the numbers tell a more complicated story. With 14 HR, 37 runs, 8 stolen bases, and a José Ramírez line that was among the most productive individual outputs in the league this week, the **Twins** posted an expected win contribution of 1.81 — only to see their actual contribution come in at 1.2. That -0.61 gap represents the worst luck delta in the league this week. The **Twins** are producing at a level that their record does not yet reflect. Over the course of a full season, talent and production tend to normalize — and if that holds, this is a roster that should be winning more than its current standing suggests.

---

## League Records

The record book required updating after Week 4, and the author of both new entries is the same.

**BaseballPro77** established a new single-week team record with 19 home runs, surpassing the previous league high and setting a benchmark that will stand as one of the defining statistical achievements of this fantasy season. The same team simultaneously set the single-week RBI record with 39, confirming what the eye test had already suggested: this was not merely a good week. It was historically good. Both records now belong to **BaseballPro77**, and both were driven in significant part by Mike Trout's six-home run, 11-RBI, .472-OBP performance — a week that belongs in any conversation about the finest individual fantasy output this league has witnessed.

---

## Manager Spotlight: Sugar Land Skeeters

There are teams in every fantasy league that don't generate the most dramatic headlines but consistently occupy the upper tier of the standings through competent, well-rounded roster construction. The **Sugar Land Skeeters** are that team in Week 4 of MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball.

Despite absorbing a 5-4 loss to the **Albuquerque Bombers** — a matchup decided largely by a temporary pitching ERA spike rather than any underlying roster deficiency — the **Skeeters** hold their position at No. 3 in the power rankings and remain the most consistent top-tier performer across all four weeks of the season. No team in the league has maintained a higher floor of production over this stretch, and the roster construction is a significant reason why.

Fernando Tatis Jr. provides elite multi-category upside at a premium position. Ian Happ offers on-base value and run production that stabilizes the lineup's middle. Austin Weathers gives the pitching staff a nightly contributor with swing-and-miss capacity. The combination of these three alone ensures that the **Skeeters** will be competitive in nearly every category on any given week. The question that this week's loss raises — whether the pitching can sustain its usual efficiency after a 6.05 ERA performance — is a legitimate one, but one week of elevated ERA numbers does not rewrite the narrative of a team that has earned its standing.

The **Skeeters'** playoff outlook is strong. Their consistency is a competitive advantage in a league where several contenders are still identifying themselves, and their roster depth provides insulation against the kind of single-week collapse that can derail a team at the standings margins.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**Mike Trout (BaseballPro77):** Trout posted six home runs, 11 RBI, and a .472 OBP this week — a performance that, by any measure, places him among the most valuable assets in the league at this moment. **Verdict: Hold.** There is no reasonable scenario in which **BaseballPro77** should be fielding offers, and any manager approaching them about Trout should expect a significant return demand. You do not sell the player who just broke two league records in a single week.

**Paul Skenes (Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs):** In his first week on the **Wonderdogs** roster following a waiver addition, Skenes delivered immediately with a 0.90 ERA and 11 strikeouts. **Verdict: Buy.** He is the most impactful waiver addition of the season to date, and any manager who can pry him away from **Wonder Wharf** — which seems unlikely given his immediate production — should do so aggressively.

**Bryan Reynolds (Buttville Skidmarks):** Reynolds' .500 OBP this week was legitimately extraordinary, yet he remains buried on a roster that cannot support his individual production. **Verdict: Sell (for the Skidmarks).** A player of Reynolds' on-base caliber should be attracting trade interest from contending managers, and the **Skidmarks** would be well-served exploring whether his value can be converted into roster help across multiple roster spots rather than concentrated in a single player surrounded by insufficient support.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **BaseballPro77** — League-record 19 HR and 39 RBI in a single week; Trout is playing like an MVP and the pitching is holding up — this is the team to beat [UP]
2. **General Hospital** — League-best 8 category wins this week, 77.1 IP, 6 QS, and a 2.68 ERA; Will Warren and a deep rotation make this team quietly terrifying [SAME]
3. **Sugar Land Skeeters** — Took a loss but remain the most consistent top-tier performer across all four weeks; Tatis, Happ, and Weathers give them multi-category upside every night [SAME]
4. **Athletic Park Dragons** — Judge's 6-HR week headlines a 5-2 win; Cristopher Sánchez and Bailey Ober both looked sharp and this pitching staff is deepening [SAME]
5. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — League-leading 8 QS and a 2.49 ERA remain the calling card; Paul Skenes' 0.90 ERA and Imanaga bouncing back make the rotation elite [SAME]
6. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** — Ramirez's .500 OBP and 4-HR week was jaw-dropping; bad luck continues to suppress the record but the talent is undeniable [UP]
7. **Hiroshima Radiation** — McLean and Eovaldi both posted 1.50 ERAs and 5 combined QS — the rotation is for real, but the offense remains the Achilles heel [DOWN]
8. **North Correa** — Chris Sale's 0.69 ERA and 16 K effort was elite; Ohtani and Hoerner give them a high floor even if the pitching outside Sale is inconsistent [SAME]
9. **Albuquerque Bombers** — Buxton led a solid offensive week and the team won 5 categories; the wave of waiver additions shows active management trying to patch real holes [SAME]
10. **Riverside Roughnecks** — Tied when they likely should have won; Langeliers, Kurtz, and Misiorowski are legitimate pieces but this team needs more consistency across the lineup [SAME]
11. **Canberra Wombats** — A 4-4 tie is better than a loss but Gunnar Henderson's .189 OBP and a thin roster ceiling remain serious concerns heading into a tough stretch [UP]
12. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** — Got torched 7-2 by the hottest team in the league; Ben Rice remains a genuine bright spot but 33 hits and a .257 OBP are roster-wide problems [DOWN]
13. **Buttville Skidmarks** — Reynolds' .500 OBP was completely wasted again as the pitching posted a 4.87 ERA; this team loses in the same way every week [SAME]
14. **Benchwarmers** — Michael King's 19 K and 1.59 ERA are genuinely great but the rest of this roster — 4 HR, 45 K, bottom-five in nearly every offensive category — is not viable without significant additions [SAME]

---

## Waiver Wire

**[A] Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — Added Paul Skenes, Ildemaro Vargas, Scott Barlow, Dustin May, Masyn Winn in a single wave. Skenes delivered immediately with a 0.90 ERA and 11 strikeouts, and Vargas contributed a .600 OBP in a bench role — this was the most impactful waiver week in the league by a considerable margin. The **Wonderdogs** were already a top-five team; this wave of additions, led by Skenes, has the potential to elevate them further. When a team adds five players and the top two contribute immediately in meaningful categories, the grade is not difficult to assign.

**[B] General Hospital** — Added Kumar Rocker (SP); dropped Jack Leiter, Janson Junk, Cristian Javier. This is precisely what intelligent roster management looks like from a team that is already winning: clear three underperforming depth arms and replace them with a single high-upside option. Rocker represents the kind of talent investment that pays dividends over the course of a full season. **General Hospital** is not scrambling — they are sharpening.

**[B] Albuquerque Bombers** — Added Keaton Winn, Noelvi Marte, Braxton Garrett, Josh Jung; dropped three roster fillers. Four additions in a single week signals a manager who understands that the current roster has real holes and is willing to be aggressive in addressing them. The quality of the individual returns remains uncertain, but the volume of activity and the intent behind it are exactly what a team sitting at No. 9 in the power rankings needs to demonstrate. The
